summary: Adversarial verification of every spoken claim in the Ep2 script

result.notes:
CONFIRMED, each element against primary or specialist sources:

1. Name + "pandemic-era": Correct name. ACP was created by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (signed Nov 15, 2021) as successor to the pandemic-relief Emergency Broadband Benefit (Dec 2020 stimulus); calling the lineage "pandemic-era" is fair colloquial framing, not misleading (corroborated via Wikipedia/FCC-cited timeline, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affordable_Connectivity_Program).

2. "Over twenty-three million households" at peak: USAC's official ACP Enrollment and Claims Tracker states 23,269,550 households were enrolled as of the Feb 8, 2024 enrollment freeze (https://www.usac.org/about/affordable-connectivity-program/acp-enrollment-and-claims-tracker/). "Over 23 million" slightly understates — acceptable direction.

3. "In June of 2024, Congress let the money run out. It ended": USAC states verbatim the program "ended on June 1, 2024, due to a lack of additional funding from Congress" (https://www.usac.org/about/affordable-connectivity-program/). Minor nuance hosts don't need: April 2024 was the last fully funded month and May 2024 was partial; the formal end date was June 1, 2024, so the spoken wording matches the official record.

4. "As of today, nothing federal has replaced it": Holds as of June 11, 2026. Broadband Breakfast's ACP coverage through May 19, 2026 confirms no enacted federal replacement; the first replacement anywhere is a STATE program (New Mexico, launched May 2026 — "New Mexico First to Replace Federal Broadband Subsidy"), and Oct 2025/Jan 2026 coverage shows advocates still pushing for a federal subsidy that hasn't materialized (https://broadbandbreakfast.com/tag/affordable-connectivity-program/).

5. Scam framing: Sound. Since enrollment froze Feb 8, 2024 and the program ended June 1, 2024, no one can sign up for ACP today, so any present-day offer of free government internet "through that program" is fraudulent by definition. FCC published ACP scam consumer guidance (fcc.gov timed out on fetch attempts, so I could not quote it directly, but the claim follows from the verified end date).

TWO CAUTIONS (not errors): (a) Federal Lifeline still exists and is active as of June 2026 — up to $9.25/month, $34.25 on Tribal lands (https://www.usac.org/lifeline/). "Nothing federal has REPLACED it" is accurate (Lifeline predates ACP), but hosts should not let it drift into "there's no federal internet help at all" — ensure the episode covers Lifeline/California LifeLine nearby. (b) Episode records July 6, 2026 — re-verify the "nothing federal has replaced it" line the week of recording in case Congress acts in late June.

Tooling note: the WebSearch tool errored on every attempt (API 400) and fcc.gov/affordableconnectivity.gov timed out or refused connections; verification relied on USAC (the program's official administrator), Broadband Breakfast trade coverage, and Wikipedia corroboration.

Every spoken figure checks out against current primary sources (verified 2026-06-11). (1) Federal Lifeline "about nine dollars": USAC's consumer site (lifelinesupport.org, fetched live) says "monthly discount of up to $9.25 on phone, internet, or bundled service." (2) "California adds... up to nineteen dollars more": CPUC's May 2026 stat sheet (cpuc.ca.gov/-/media/.../lifeline-stat-sheet-051126.pdf) states "up to a $19.00 discount from CA LifeLine in addition to up to a $9.25 discount from Federal LifeLine"; same figures in californialifeline.com's own site strings (May 17, 2026 archive). (3) "Almost thirty dollars": CPUC's Feb 2026 fact sheet "California LifeLine vs. Federal Lifeline" (cpuc.ca.gov/-/media/.../california-lifeline-vs-federal-lifeline-021726.pdf) confirms "total combined subsidy: up to $28.25 per month" — "almost thirty" understates, which is fine. (4) Carriers: TruConnect's live California page (truconnect.com/states/california) advertises a FREE CA LifeLine plan with unlimited voice/text, 6GB data + hotspot, and a "FREE Android Smartphone"; homepage says "No contracts, no hidden fees, no credit checks." Assurance Wireless is still active (site live today though bot-blocked; Wayback snapshots of the homepage May 10, 2026 and the CA page Mar 15, 2026): free CA-enhanced plan with 12GB high-speed data, unlimited talk & text, "Free Phone or Bring Your Own," and "No credit check. No annual contracts. No activation fees." So "no bill, no contract, no credit check" is each carrier's own wording. Three watch items, none rising to a script error: (a) PROCESS CHANGE — as of Feb 1, 2026, California LifeLine and federal Lifeline require two SEPARATE enrollments (CPUC fact sheet; Assurance's CA page repeats it). "Stack them" is still true, but consider one added line telling viewers they now apply to both programs (the carrier walks them through it) so nobody gets a partial subsidy or break in service. (b) The FCC opened a rulemaking on federal Lifeline Feb 18, 2026 (docs.fcc.gov/public/attachments/DOC-418282A1.pdf); CPUC states no changes to either discount as of now, but re-verify the $9.25 figure the week before air. (c) Pedantic: voice-ONLY service gets $5.25 federal, not $9.25 — irrelevant for these data-bundled smartphone plans, but don't generalize the $9.25 to landline-only voice. Also note TruConnect's CA data allotment is 6GB; "a pile of data" leans on Assurance's 12GB, but as a characterization it's defensible.

All four spoken elements verified against primary sources. (1) Income limits: CPUC's California LifeLine eligibility page (https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/consumer-support/financial-assistance-savings-and-discounts/lifeline/california-lifeline-eligibility) lists, for the program year effective June 1, 2026 - May 31, 2027, household of 1 = $24,600 and household of 4 = $50,800 (rows are per-household-size: 1/$24,600, 2/$33,300, 3/$42,100, 4/$50,800, +$8,700 each additional). These figures are in force both at record (July 6, 2026) and on air; spoken wording matches exactly and "about" hedges correctly. (2) Program-based eligibility: same CPUC page lists "Medicaid/Medi-Cal", "CalFresh, Food Stamps or Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)", "Women, Infants and Children Program (WIC)", and "National School Lunch Program (NSL)" as qualifying programs. "Qualify automatically" reads as categorical eligibility, which is correct; proof of enrollment is still required at application, and the script's next beat sends viewers to apply, so no actionable harm. (3) Application path: CPUC's main LifeLine page says to call a participating phone company to start the application and points to "Provider Search" at www.californialifeline.com - matches the spoken instruction. (4) Two applications since February: CPUC announcement "California LifeLine vs Federal Lifeline" (https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/news-and-updates/all-news/california-lifeline-vs-federal-lifeline) states "as of February 1, 2026" the two programs "require two separate enrollment processes" and "New subscribers must enroll separately in each program" (federal via www.lifelinesupport.org / National Verifier, state via www.californialifeline.com). Backed by the FCC WCB order revoking California's Lifeline opt-out status (Nov 20, 2025, https://www.fcc.gov/document/wcb-revokes-lifeline-california-opt-out). Note for producers, not a fix: existing dual-enrollees keep both discounts until their annual renewal, when they must renew each separately; and WIC/free school lunch qualify a household for the STATE discount only - the federal Lifeline standard list (https://www.lifelinesupport.org/how-to-qualify/) is SNAP, Medicaid, SSI, Federal Public Housing Assistance, and Veterans Pension (or income at/below 135% FPG). The script as written doesn't claim WIC/lunch unlocks the federal form, but hosts should avoid implying it ad-lib: a WIC-only household gets the up-to-$19/mo state discount but not the $9.25/mo federal add-on.

Confirmed all five spoken claims. (1) One-per-household: USAC states "Only one Lifeline benefit is allowed per household" (https://www.usac.org/lifeline/consumer-eligibility/); the benefit applies to phone or internet (or a bundle), not stacked — matches "one, total, phone or internet, not both." California LifeLine likewise allows one discount per household (https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/consumer-support/financial-assistance-savings-and-discounts/lifeline); narrow exception: TTY users / Deaf and Disabled Telecommunications Program participants may get two — the script understates for that tiny group, which is the safe direction. (2) Usage rule: 47 CFR 54.405(e)(3) — de-enrollment after 30 consecutive days of non-usage on plans with no monthly fee, with a 15-day notice/cure window (https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/47/54.405). Script's "use it at least once a month or they shut it off" is slightly stricter than reality (viewers actually get a 15-day cure after notice), so it errs conservative; correctly scoped to "the free plan." (3) Annual recertification: USAC — recertification is an annual requirement, 60-day response window, automatic de-enrollment on failure (https://www.usac.org/lifeline/national-verifier/recertification/); LifelineSupport FAQ: "Every year, USAC or your state... will check to confirm that you still qualify" (https://www.lifelinesupport.org/faqs/). (4) T-Mobile: Assurance Wireless is T-Mobile-owned and has used the T-Mobile network since 2020 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assurance_Wireless); TruConnect is an MVNO on T-Mobile and Verizon networks (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TruConnect). These are the dominant free-phone CA LifeLine brands, so the hedged "Most of them use T-Mobile" holds, and the actionable advice ("ask which network") is sound. (5) Landline: CPUC — "California LifeLine provides discounted home phone and cell phone services to qualified households"; federal voice-only support of $5.25/mo is confirmed available through November 30, 2026 per USAC's minimum-service-standards page citing the FCC Wireline Competition Bureau's July 1, 2025 phase-down pause, order DA-25-567A1 (https://www.usac.org/lifeline/rules-and-requirements/minimum-service-standards/). The July 6, 2026 record date and near-term air window fall inside that confirmed period, and the CA state landline discount continues regardless. Minor production caveat (no script change needed): if the episode re-airs after Nov 30, 2026, the federal voice-only piece depends on another FCC pause; the spoken line names no dollar amount or date, so it remains safe as worded. Note: WebSearch tool was non-functional in this environment (API error on every call); verification was done via direct WebFetch of USAC, FCC/CFR (Cornell LII mirror), CPUC, and Wikipedia carrier pages.

All spoken facts check out against current primary sources (WebSearch tool was broken in this environment; verification done via Wayback Machine captures of Comcast's own pages — including a capture from 2026-06-05, six days ago — plus live fetches).

CONFIRMED:
1. "$14.95 a month, 75 Mbps" — internetessentials.com homepage, archived 2026-06-05: "Home internet starting at $14.95/mo" and FAQ: "$14.95/mo for up to 75 Mbps, or $29.95/mo for up to 100 Mbps [IE Plus]" (https://web.archive.org/web/20260605215224/https://www.internetessentials.com/). No "+tax" qualifier on the internet price. Note the page's own hedge is "up to 75 Mbps"; spoken wording drops "up to" but matches Comcast's own headline ("Internet Essentials 75 Mbps").
2. "Free equipment" — internetessentials.com/apply FAQ, archived 2026-04-08: "A Getting Started Kit with your Xfinity WiFi Gateway... will either be shipped to your home at no cost or available for pickup at an Xfinity Store"; technician install "at no cost" if home isn't self-install eligible (https://web.archive.org/web/20260408055435/https://www.internetessentials.com/apply). Program rules: "Self-Install kit included at no extra charge" (https://web.archive.org/web/20241127184402/https://www.internetessentials.com/termsandconditions).
3. "No contract" — Comcast press release (Apr 2024 speed-increase announcement): no fixed-term contract, no credit check, no shipping charge ("No habrá contrato de plazo fijo, ni verificación de crédito, ni cargos por envío") (https://web.archive.org/web/20240424085758/https://corporate.comcast.com/press/releases/comcast-aumenta-el-acceso-y-la-velocidad-de-internet-essentials); no term/ETF anywhere in the current program rules.
4. Eligibility programs — apply page (2026-04 capture) accepts: Medicaid (=Medi-Cal), SNAP (=CalFresh), NSLP/Head Start, WIC, plus TANF, SSI, housing assistance, LIHEAP, Pell Grant, VA pension, tribal assistance, or income ≤200% FPL. "CalFresh, Medi-Cal, school lunch, W-I-C, and more" is exactly right.
5. Grass Valley/Nevada City = Comcast territory — Xfinity local pages exist and market home internet for both: xfinity.com/local/ca/grass-valley (archived 2025-12-07, https://web.archive.org/web/20251207181849/https://www.xfinity.com/local/ca/grass-valley) and xfinity.com/local/ca/nevada-city (archived 2026-03-16).
6. Truckee = Optimum, not Comcast — BroadbandNow Truckee (fetched live 2026-06-11): Optimum cable covers 95.1% of Truckee; Comcast/Xfinity absent from Truckee provider list (https://broadbandnow.com/California/Truckee). Optimum is the 2022 Altice rebrand of Suddenlink, matching the research note.

CAVEATS producers should know (not errors in the spoken text, but burn risks if hosts ad-lib "anyone can get this"):
- You CANNOT be a current Xfinity Internet customer — must have had no Xfinity Internet in the last 90 days, and no Comcast debt less than 1 year old (eligibility rules on both the 2026-06-05 homepage and 2026-04-08 apply captures). A viewer already paying full-price Xfinity can't simply downgrade to IE. Consider adding a half-line: "it's for households new to Xfinity."
- Homepage fine print: "Professional installation extra" and price "applies to a single outlet" — self-install kit is the free path (and per the apply FAQ, tech install is free only where self-install isn't offered).
- Outlying Nevada County (beyond the Grass Valley/Nevada City town cores) often has no Comcast plant; "check your address" framing is safest.

CONFIRMED against nevcofiber.com (primary source; site of Nevada County Fiber, Inc., branded "NevCoFiber" in page title and domain): (1) "Community Assist Program - Internet for $15/month" — verbatim "internet service for just $15/month to qualifying households," qualify via income guidelines (1040 line 11) or enrollment in a government assistance program; company self-funds the discount post-ACP. (2) It is fiber, and "There are no bandwidth limits" (no data caps). (3) PSPS claim: Dec 2021 site update says "a robust automated power system for internet access through all PG&E power outages," even multi-week ones — supports "stays up when PG&E shuts the power off," though the site says "automated power system," not specifically "battery backup," and this is the company's self-claim. Viewers should also know their own home router still needs power at home during a shutoff. (4) Coverage area: Banner Mountain, Red Dog Road, Banner Lava Cap, Buckeye Rd, etc. — consistent with "east of Nevada City." (5) "Building more right now": Nov 2024 update — "We have just begun construction on our next grant for 215 homes on Banner Mountain," target "all 215 homes connected by December 2026," plus a 280-connection grant application pending. (6) nevcofiber.com resolves and is the correct URL.

OFF: The reach figure. The script says "a few hundred homes... so far." The site's own banner says "We have 100+ connections working, hundreds more to come!" — i.e., 100-something connected now, with the 215-home build not due to finish until December 2026 (after the July 6, 2026 recording). "A few hundred so far" overstates the documented count roughly 2-3x; no public source supports it. Per the overstatement rule, this must come down to what's documented.

Sources: https://www.nevcofiber.com (homepage: Community Assist, $15/mo, no bandwidth limits, 100+ connections, PG&E outage power system, Nov 2024 215-home grant build, Dec 2026 target, © Nevada County Fiber, Inc); https://www.nevcofiber.com/blog (Nov 2023 post: community ISP founded 2019 in Nevada County). Caveats: WebSearch tooling errored in this environment (API 400, retried), so verification used direct fetches; CPUC FFA award list page 404'd, so the grant detail rests on the company's own published statement — adequate for the script's soft "building more right now" wording. The "100+ connections" banner is undated, so the true July 2026 count could be higher — but it cannot be asserted as "a few hundred" on air; producers could confirm a current number directly with NevCoFiber before taping and upgrade the line if documented.
result.scriptFix: Replace the reach line with: "more than a hundred homes around Banner Mountain and Red Dog Road so far — and they're building out a couple hundred more right now." (Recommended, not mandatory: in the prior sentence, say "their network has backup power, so it stays up when P-G-and-E shuts the power off" — the company says "automated power system," not specifically batteries — and consider adding "you'll still need power at your house to run your router.")

result.notes: CONFIRMED: (1) Nevada County's Last-Mile Federal Funding Account allocation is $74,866,197.58 — CPUC D.22-04-055, Table 1 Rural County Allocations (https://docs.cpuc.ca.gov/PublishedDocs/Published/G000/M470/K543/470543650.PDF). (2) "One of the biggest rural pots" holds: 2nd-largest of 27 rural-county allocations, behind El Dorado ($111.9M); Tehama is #3 at $74.8M. (3) Crews are building: Nevada County Fiber's site (https://www.nevcofiber.com/) says construction on its 215-home Banner Mountain FFA project began Nov 2024; SR-89 middle-mile fiber construction from I-80 in Nevada County runs spring 2025–fall 2026 (Mountain Messenger, May 19, 2026, https://www.themountainmessenger.org/article/broadband-work-continues-on-highway-89-between-sierraville-and-truckee). (4) LifeLine amounts are exact: $20/mo standalone broadband, $30/mo bundled ($20+$10), plus up to $39/yr connection reimbursement — D.25-08-050 (https://docs.cpuc.ca.gov/PublishedDocs/Published/G000/M578/K668/578668809.PDF). OFF: (A) Present-tense "has about $75M earmarked... and crews are already building" overstates. Resolution T-17835 (Aug 22, 2024, https://docs.cpuc.ca.gov/PublishedDocs/Published/G000/M538/K700/538700446.PDF) awarded Nevada County only $14,740,272 total — PST Truckee $8,061,025 (220 unserved locations) and Nevada County Fiber Banner Mountain $6,679,247 (215 locations) — "which leaves a remainder of $60,125,926." FFA Round 1 then closed Feb 20, 2025; the 2025 limited application window is restricted to six counties NOT including Nevada, and the next last-mile money ($550M) is slated only for the 2027-28 state budget (https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/internet-and-phone/broadband-implementation-for-california/last-mile-federal-funding-account). So ~$60M of the "earmark" is not currently funding anything; only ~435 locations are funded. A viewer in an unserved home outside Truckee/Banner Mountain could wrongly expect funded buildout. Also "state dollars" is loose — the Federal Funding Account is largely federal ARPA money in a state-administered account (SB 156). (B) "The state just voted... rolling out through this year" is stale: the CPUC voted Aug 28, 2025 (~11 months before the July 6, 2026 recording), and the Home Broadband Pilot ALREADY LAUNCHED Jan 26, 2026 with 11 ISPs registered including Comcast/Xfinity and Charter/Spectrum (CPUC release Jan 26, 2026, https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/news-and-updates/all-news/making-home-internet-more-affordable-for-californians; CA Broadband for All Feb 2026 update, https://broadbandforall.cdt.ca.gov/2026/02/03/broadband-for-all-update-february-2026/; live provider pages at spectrum.net, xfinity.com, caltel.com). Viewers can enroll now — saying it's merely "rolling out" undersells the actionable benefit while "just voted" misstates timing.
result.scriptFix: "Nevada County got one of the biggest rural broadband allocations in California -- almost seventy-five million dollars set aside by the state in 2022 -- and so far nearly fifteen million of that has turned into two local fiber projects, with crews already building. Second: since this January, that LifeLine discount also applies to home internet -- up to twenty or thirty dollars a month off -- through participating providers like Xfinity and Spectrum, and more are signing on this year."

result.notes: Verified live (2026-06-11) against the primary source, Nevada County Library "Hotspots" page (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/3934/Hotspots). CONFIRMED: free with "unlimited access to the internet"; carriers listed exactly as Verizon, T-Mobile, AT&T; "Loan period is 14 days. Hotspots are not renewable."; "can connect to up to 10 devices at one time"; Adult and Teen cardholders only (Youth, Limited, Educator cards restricted); waiver must be signed in person at a library location; "Overdue hotspots will be disabled remotely"; account "charged for the $200 dollar replacement cost. Upon return of the device, the charge will be cleared." The page's "Request a hotspot" button links to the Polaris online catalog (library.nevadacountyca.gov/polaris/...), confirming holds are placed via the catalog. The Library Cards & Borrowing Policies page (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/3494) confirms "everyone is eligible for a free library card" and lists Hotspots under 2-week loans, consistent with 14 days. OFF: (1) Actionable omission — the Hotspots page states "Teens under the age of 18 must have a parent or guardian sign the Hotspot and Recreation Collection waiver." The script tells teen cardholders to just "sign a one-page form at the desk once"; a teen arriving alone would be turned away. (2) Unverified specifics — neither "one-page" nor "once" (sign one time, on file forever) appears in any official source; the waiver document is not published online, so both are overstated certainty. Also note (no fix needed since script already scopes to adult/teen): the free card for non-residents is a Limited account, which CANNOT borrow hotspots; Adult cards require proof of a California (or Washoe County, NV) address — fine for the Nevada County audience.
result.scriptFix: Adult or teen cardholder, sign a quick waiver at the desk — and teens under eighteen, bring a parent or guardian to sign it with you. Then place holds in the catalog, the box shuts off if it's late, and the $200 charge clears as soon as you return it. The library card itself? Free.

result.notes:
CONFIRMED against official Nevada County sources (county site blocks generic fetchers; verified via direct retrieval of nevadacountyca.gov pages and the library's LibCal):
1) WiFi: "Free public WiFi is available at all library locations, and can be accessed from outside the building 24/7" — official policy page (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/2118/Computers-Printing-WiFi). "Parking lot, twenty-four hours a day" is a fair colloquial rendering of "outside the building 24/7," and "every branch" matches "all library locations."
2) Free computers: same page — public computers at all locations, no charge, two one-hour sessions/day with card or guest pass. Accurate.
3) Chromebooks: in-library use confirmed — "Chromebooks and Macbooks are available for in-library use for two hour time blocks" (same page) and "available for in-house use at several Nevada County Libraries... must be 12 or older" (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/2168/Check-Out-a-Chromebook-or-Laptop). Note: "several" libraries, not all; script doesn't claim all, so OK.
4) After-hours: Open+ "is currently only available at the Bear River Library and the Penn Valley Library" — branches correct (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/3197/Open-Access). BUT the spoken line "your card unlocks the door" omits hard prerequisites: 18+, adult library card in good standing, completing a Terms & Conditions application form, AND an in-person orientation at the branch BEFORE the card+PIN will open the door. A viewer who drives over after hours with only a library card will be locked out. This is the actionable overstatement requiring a fix. Open+ questions: Bear River (530) 265-7052, Penn Valley (530) 432-5764.
5) 1:1 tech help: LibCal confirms free one-on-one appointments — "Meet one on one with knowledgeable staff or an expert volunteer for assistance with tech help, legal matters and more" — but only bookable at Madelyn Helling Library and Truckee Library (https://nevadacountyca.libcal.com/appointments); Tech Drop In also confirmed (e.g., Fridays 1-3 PM, CTC Classroom, Madelyn Helling). Two soft overstatements worth smoothing: it may be a trained volunteer rather than "a real librarian," and within a cluster opened by "Every branch has...", viewers may assume 1:1 help is at every branch when it's two locations.
6) Hours change: official Locations & Hours page states "Upcoming changes for the Penn Valley and Bear River Library operating hours — Effective July 1, 2026" with new schedules; Truckee also changes (Saturdays go to CLOSED) (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/297/Locations-Hours). Shawna's "branch hours just changed at the start of July — check the hours before you drive over" is accurate for a July 6 record date and good advice. Reference phone 530-265-7050 confirmed on the same page and LibCal footer.
No wrong numbers or stale facts found; the only viewer-burn risk is the Open+ door-access wording (plus the minor librarian/branch softening folded into the fix).
result.scriptFix: "Every branch has free WiFi that works from the PARKING LOT, twenty-four hours a day... Free computers inside. Chromebooks you can use in the building. Penn Valley and Bear River even have after-hours access for adults -- you sign up once and do a quick orientation at the branch, and after that your library card unlocks the door... And free one-on-one tech help at the Madelyn Helling and Truckee libraries. A real person will sit down with you... by appointment."

result.notes: CONFIRMED — Computers for Classrooms (Chico): Official price sheet (https://computersforclassrooms.org/buy-a-computer/price-sheet/, prices effective 6/25/2025) lists the desktop at $150 (HP EliteDesk 800 G5, i5-9500, 16GB/480GB NVMe + 1TB backup) and states each desktop comes with LCD, keyboard, mouse, cables, Windows, and a one-year warranty; all systems include Windows 11 + Microsoft Office 2019. Policies page (https://computersforclassrooms.org/policies-warranties/) independently confirms desktops carry a one-year hardware-and-software warranty — the 'ONE-YEAR warranty' line is safe to air as written for desktops. Laptops are $125 (i7 8th gen) and $175 (i5 10th gen), so 'around the same price' holds. Shipping confirmed: orders ship UPS Ground same day, California residents only (https://computersforclassrooms.org/buy-a-computer/computers-for-low-income-families/) — fine for a Nevada County, CA audience. CONFIRMED — human-I-T: store (https://store.human-i-t.org/basic-chromebook-laptop/) lists the Get-it-Done Chromebook at $129.99 regular / $89.99 Gold Member, so 'around ninety to a hundred thirty' is accurate; hotspot service page (https://human-i-t.org/low-cost-internet/) confirms unlimited 5G internet at $14.99/mo, no contract, matching 'about fifteen dollars a month,' and the 'if you've got cell signal' framing is correct since it is a cellular hotspot. THE PROBLEM — the $15/mo line omits that the service requires buying the TCL LINKZONE 5G hotspot for $75 one-time (bundle with first month is $87.98; alternative is a 4G hotspot rental at $10/mo on top of the $14.99). A viewer budgeting fifteen dollars would hit an ~$88 first charge — an affordability overstatement, so the hotspot sentence needs the one-time device cost added (fix below). MINOR CAVEATS (no fix required, but useful for hosts/show notes): (1) CFC laptops carry only a 90-day battery/hardware warranty (one year on hard drive and software) — the script's one-year claim is attached to the desktop sentence and the laptop sentence claims only price, so it's technically accurate, but hosts should not extend 'one-year warranty' to laptops if ad-libbing; (2) both programs are eligibility-gated: CFC requires meeting 1 of 15 qualifying categories (low-income qualifies) plus a $40 non-refundable deposit that counts toward the price, and human-I-T's $89.99 Gold price and $14.99 internet both require documented proof of ≤200% FPL income or enrollment in SNAP/Medicaid/SSI/Lifeline/etc. — the show's audience qualifies, but verification paperwork is required; (3) CFC warranty repairs must be brought or shipped back to Chico (no house calls); (4) CFC price sheet is dated 6/25/2025 — re-spot-check the week before the July 6, 2026 record.
result.scriptFix: "...and if you've got cell signal at home, they offer unlimited hotspot internet for about fifteen dollars a month -- plus about seventy-five dollars, one time, for the hotspot device itself."

result.notes: CONFIRMED: (1) Senior Planet from AARP — hotline page live and current (modified 2026-06-06): "Call the Senior Planet Hotline at 888-713-3495 for free tech support from patient trainers" (their literal copy matches the script's "patient trainers"); free; hours now Mon-Fri 9am-8pm ET PLUS Sat 9am-2pm ET (research notes' "Mon-Fri" is stale, but hours aren't spoken, so no on-air impact). Free live online classes over Zoom confirmed ("more free online programs"). 60+ framing matches Senior Planet's target audience; the hotline itself has no hard age gate, so "(60+)" understates — acceptable. Sources: https://seniorplanet.org/hotline/ (content via cms.seniorplanet.org WordPress API), https://seniorplanet.org/classes/. (2) California Connect (CPUC DDTP) — "no income test at all" is verbatim-supported: FAQ says "no age or income requirements associated with the programs." Free amplified phones (Clarity cordless amplified w/ large buttons), big-button, and captioned phones (CapTel 840 Plus) all confirmed offered at no cost; 1-800-806-1191 confirmed. Sources: https://caconnect.org/faqs/, https://caconnect.org/hearing/. (3) 211 Connecting Point — footer: "OPEN 24/7 • FREE • CONFIDENTIAL"; "Para obtener ayuda en español, llame al 2-1-1"; Nevada County explicitly served; dial 2-1-1 works. Source: https://211connectingpoint.org/. (4) 988 — "Call, text, or chat… anytime, day or night… free and confidential," 24/7/365; call/text need no internet. Source: https://988lifeline.org/. Minor nuance (no fix needed): texting requires an SMS-capable phone with service, and unlike 911, a phone with no active service plan may not reach 988. (5) findhelp.org is operational (Cloudflare anti-bot blocked deep fetch, HTTP 403 challenge — site up); techempower.org returns HTTP 200. OFF: the California Connect line overstates ease of access. Eligibility requires CA residency, existing phone service, AND a disability certification signed by an authorized professional (MD/PA/NP, audiologist, optometrist, etc. — FAQ: certification "must be signed by an authorized Certifying Agent"); equipment is also a loan ("on loan to you until you can no longer benefit"), one phone per person. The current site never says "ships"; post-approval you call the Contact Center to be matched with equipment (service-center visit optional, so remote fulfillment is the default — "ships" is defensible but undocumented). A viewer hearing only "no income test at all" could call expecting a phone with no qualifying step and be surprised by the professional sign-off requirement — that's the burn risk. Sources: https://caconnect.org/faqs/, https://caconnect.org/apply/ (application includes Professional Consent and Signature section).
result.scriptFix: ships you a free adapted phone -- amplified, big-button, captioned -- no income test at all; you just need a doctor or other licensed professional to sign off that you need it.
